greenstalkNotes: Trailing habit cascades beautifully from top tiers. Elevated position protects from slugs.

varieties.name: Cambridge Favourite
varieties.notes: RHS top pick for family gardens, reliable cropper

notes: Plant at top tiers to keep fruit away from slugs. Perfect picking height for children. Space 35-45cm apart in ground.
kidActivity: Children love searching among the leaves to find bright, treasure-like ripe fruits.

inGround.commonProblems: Bird damage to ripe fruit. Net with fine mesh.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Determinate trailing variety bred for containers. Will not snap pockets like indeterminate types.
notes: Must use determinate/trailing varieties in GreenStalks. Standard indeterminate tomatoes will snap pockets.

inGround.feeding: Liquid tomato feed weekly once first truss sets
inGround.commonProblems: Blight in wet summers. Water consistently.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact root system fits perfectly in pockets. Plant alongside tomatoes for classic companion planting.

varieties.name: Greek Dwarf
varieties.notes: Compact mound, ideal for small pockets

notes: Tuck into the same pocket as tomatoes. Improves tomato flavour and repels whiteflies.

inGround.commonProblems: Frost tender. Do not plant out until June.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact and highly productive in 10-inch pockets with zero support needed.

varieties.name: Purple Teepee
varieties.notes: Purple pods easy to spot for picking

notes: Lower tiers work well. Fixes nitrogen in soil which benefits neighbouring plants.

inGround.commonProblems: Slugs attack seedlings.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Cut-and-come-again harvesting maximises yield from a single pocket.

varieties.name: Perpetual Spinach
varieties.notes: Hardy, bolts less than true spinach

notes: Snap off outer leaves only for continuous harvests. Thrives in cooler Surrey spring.

inGround.commonProblems: Bolts in hot dry weather.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Colourful stems add visual interest. Cut-and-come-again harvesting works perfectly.
notes: Closely related to perpetual spinach. Stunning rainbow stems from Bright Lights variety.

inGround.commonProblems: Generally trouble-free.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: GreenStalk drainage mimics Mediterranean conditions these woody herbs prefer.
notes: Evergreen perennial. Will persist across seasons in a GreenStalk if protected from hard frost.

inGround.commonProblems: Replace every 3-4 years.

soil.notes: Mediterranean herbs prefer lean alkaline soil with good drainage.

greenstalkNotes: Thrives in the well-drained conditions of vertical planters.
notes: Hardy perennial. Excellent drainage in GreenStalk suits it perfectly.

inGround.commonProblems: Needs good drainage.

soil.notes: Mediterranean herbs prefer lean alkaline soil with good drainage.

greenstalkNotes: Deeper tap root than other herbs but works well in middle tiers.
notes: Biennial - will bolt in second year. Replace annually for best results.

inGround.commonProblems: Slow to germinate (3-4 weeks).

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Trailing habit cascades from bottom tiers. Sacrificial trap crop draws aphids away from vegetables.
notes: Entirely edible — flowers and leaves have a peppery, watercress-like kick. Large seeds are easy for small hands. Sow in poor soil; rich soil produces leaves at the expense of flowers. Acts as trap crop for aphids.
kidActivity: Children can pick and eat flowers and leaves straight from the plant. Large seeds are easy for small hands to sow directly.
soilTipSurrey: Sow directly after late April in poor soil for best flowering. Rich soil produces more leaves than flowers.

inGround.commonProblems: Aphids expected — acts as trap crop drawing them from other plants.

soil.notes: Thrives in poor soil — rich soil produces leaves at the expense of flowers.

greenstalkNotes: Must use dwarf varieties like Cupid. Standard sweet peas need climbing support and are unsuitable.

varieties.name: Bijou Mix
varieties.notes: Bushy, no support needed, mixed colours

notes: Standard sweet peas need vertical nets - only dwarf varieties work in GreenStalks. Beautiful fragrance.

inGround.commonProblems: Mice eat seeds. Soak overnight before sowing.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Shallow roots perfect for pockets. Cut-and-come-again loose leaf varieties maximise harvests.
notes: Succession sow every 2-3 weeks for continuous supply. Benefits from partial shade in midsummer.

inGround.commonProblems: Slugs are the main enemy. Use beer traps or nematodes.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact clumps fit perfectly. Purple flowers are edible and attract pollinators.
notes: Perennial - comes back each year. Excellent companion to deter aphids. Grows well in both full sun and partial shade.
kidActivity: Children love the purple pom-pom flowers and can snip the grass-like leaves with child-safe scissors for cooking.

inGround.commonProblems: Cut back after flowering to encourage fresh growth.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: GreenStalk pockets naturally contain mint's invasive runners - actually an advantage here.

varieties.name: Spearmint
varieties.notes: Classic mint for drinks and cooking

notes: Invasive in ground - GreenStalk pockets actually contain it well. Keep separate from other herbs.

inGround.commonProblems: Extremely invasive — always grow in containers or sunken pots.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact habit perfect for pockets. Strong scent deters whitefly and other pests.
notes: Classic companion plant. Strong scent deters whitefly, aphids, and nematodes.

inGround.commonProblems: Deadhead regularly for continuous flowering.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Fast crop - can fit 6-8 radishes per pocket. Great for filling gaps between slower plants.
notes: Fastest vegetable - harvest in 4 weeks. Sow in gaps between transplants for bonus crops.

inGround.commonProblems: Flea beetle makes small holes in leaves but rarely serious.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Shallow roots ideal for pockets. Cut-and-come-again for weeks of harvests.
notes: Best in cooler months - bolts quickly in midsummer heat. Shade from upper tiers can help.

inGround.commonProblems: Bolts quickly in hot weather. Sow successionally.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Slim upright habit means you can pack 8-10 per pocket. Allium scent deters pests.
notes: Pack densely in pockets. The allium family scent helps deter carrot fly and aphids.

inGround.commonProblems: Avoid where alliums grew recently.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Dwarf varieties fit well. Larger types may outgrow pockets - harvest frequently to manage size.
notes: Very hardy - improves with frost. Use dwarf varieties in GreenStalks.

inGround.commonProblems: Net against pigeons and butterflies.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact dwarf varieties work well. Needs warmest position possible in Surrey.

varieties.name: Apache
varieties.notes: Dwarf, bred for containers, medium heat

notes: Start indoors early (January-February) for UK growing season. Needs maximum sun.

inGround.commonProblems: Needs maximum warmth. Grow under glass in cool summers.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Vigorous climber reaching 2m+ needs strong cane support. Far too tall and heavy for pockets.

varieties.name: Hestia
varieties.notes: Dwarf bushy variety, no support needed

notes: Needs 2m cane wigwam or frame. Pick regularly to keep cropping. Frost tender — do not sow outdoors until late May in Surrey.

inGround.commonProblems: Needs 2m cane support. Flowers drop in hot dry weather.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Tall stems (90cm+) and deep tap roots make this unsuitable for pockets. Better in ground or deep raised beds.

varieties.name: Aquadulce Claudia
varieties.notes: Best for autumn/early spring sowing, very hardy

varieties.name: The Sutton
varieties.notes: Dwarf variety at 30cm, good for containers

notes: One of the earliest crops. Autumn sow Aquadulce in November for May harvest. Pinch out tips when first pods set to deter blackfly.

inGround.commonProblems: Pinch out tips when pods set to reduce blackfly.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Most varieties need support netting and reach 1m+. Only dwarf varieties could work but yields are low.

varieties.name: Tom Thumb
varieties.notes: True dwarf at 25cm, no support needed

notes: Succession sow every 3 weeks March-June for continuous harvest. Mice love the seeds — start in guttering indoors.

inGround.commonProblems: Mice steal seeds. Use traps or cover with fleece.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Enormous leaves and 90cm spread make this impossible in pockets. Needs ground or very large containers.

varieties.name: Black Beauty
varieties.notes: Dark green, classic variety, heavy cropper

notes: Incredibly productive — two plants can feed a family. Harvest at 15cm for best flavour. Sow on edge to prevent rot.

inGround.commonProblems: Harvest courgettes at 15cm for best flavour.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Vigorous vining habit and large leaf canopy far too expansive for pockets. Needs trellis or greenhouse.

varieties.name: Marketmore
varieties.notes: Outdoor ridge type, reliable in UK

notes: Outdoor ridge types best for Surrey. Sow indoors on edge in April, plant out after last frost. Keep well watered.

inGround.commonProblems: Keep consistently moist. Do not let roots dry out.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Baby beet varieties work well in pockets. Pull at golf-ball size for best results in limited depth.
notes: Soak seed clusters overnight before sowing. Each cluster produces 2-3 seedlings — thin to strongest. Leaves also edible.

inGround.feeding: No additional feeding on fertile soil
inGround.commonProblems: Soak seed clusters overnight before sowing for better germination.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Short-rooted varieties essential for pockets. Elevated position helps deter carrot fly which flies low.

varieties.name: Paris Market
varieties.notes: Round roots ideal for shallow containers

notes: Use short-rooted types in containers. Sow thinly to avoid thinning (which attracts carrot fly). Companion plant with onion family.

inGround.commonProblems: Cover with fine mesh against carrot fly. Companion-plant with alliums.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Deep tap root (30cm+) far exceeds pocket depth. Must be grown in ground or very deep raised beds.
notes: Always use fresh seed — viability drops drastically after one year. Sow February onwards in Surrey. Flavour improves after frost.

inGround.commonProblems: Use fresh seed each year — viability drops rapidly.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Requires earthing up and deep soil (30cm+). Pockets far too shallow. Use potato bags or ground planting.
notes: Chit tubers from late January. Plant in Surrey mid-March, protect from late frost with fleece. Harvest when flowers open.

inGround.commonProblems: Blight in wet summers. Earth up regularly.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Needs deep earthing up and 38cm spacing. Far too large and deep-rooting for pockets.

varieties.name: Desiree
varieties.notes: Red skin, good all-rounder, blight tolerant

notes: Plant mid-April in Surrey. Earth up regularly. Leave in ground until foliage dies back. Cure for 2 weeks before storing.

inGround.commonProblems: More blight-vulnerable than earlies. Sarpo varieties resist blight.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Sets work in pockets if planted shallowly. Slim upright habit fits well. Harvest when tops fall over.

varieties.name: Stuttgarter Giant
varieties.notes: Reliable, flat globe shape, stores well

varieties.name: Red Baron
varieties.notes: Deep red, mild flavour, good raw

notes: Sets far easier than seed. Plant with tips just showing. Do not damage necks when weeding. Dry thoroughly before storing.

inGround.commonProblems: White rot persists 15+ years in soil.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact upright habit fits pockets well. Needs cold period so plant autumn for best results.

varieties.name: Elephant Garlic
varieties.notes: Huge mild cloves, actually a leek relative

notes: Plant individual cloves October-February. Needs cold period for bulb formation. Harvest when lower leaves yellow. Dry for 3 weeks.

inGround.commonProblems: Needs cold period (vernalisation) for bulb formation. Plant Oct-Nov.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Needs deep planting and earthing up for white stems. Pocket depth insufficient for blanching.
notes: Transplant seedlings into deep dibbed holes. Fill with water not soil. Extremely hardy — stands in ground all winter in Surrey.

inGround.commonProblems: Plant in deep dibbed holes. Do not fill — let soil wash in.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Grows to 2m tall with extensive root system. Must be planted in blocks for wind pollination.

varieties.name: Swift
varieties.notes: Extra-early F1, suited to UK climate

notes: Plant in blocks (minimum 4x4) not rows for wind pollination. Harvest when tassels turn brown and juice is milky.

inGround.commonProblems: Plant in blocks (not rows) for wind pollination.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Very large plant (60cm+ spread) that occupies ground for 9 months. Far too big for pockets.
notes: Sow spring, harvest following winter/spring. Occupies ground for 9+ months. Worth it for hungry gap production in Feb-April.

inGround.commonProblems: Occupies ground 9+ months. Net against pigeons.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Large plant requiring 60cm spacing and very rich, firm soil. Not feasible in pockets.

varieties.name: All The Year Round
varieties.notes: Versatile, sow at intervals for succession

varieties.name: Graffiti
varieties.notes: Purple heads, striking colour, good flavour

notes: Most demanding brassica. Needs very firm, rich soil and consistent watering. Protect curds by bending leaves over them.

inGround.commonProblems: Most demanding brassica. Needs consistent moisture and feeding.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Large heads need 45cm+ spacing and deep root run. Pockets too small and shallow.

varieties.name: Hispi
varieties.notes: Pointed spring cabbage, quick to mature

varieties.name: January King
varieties.notes: Extremely hardy savoy type, stands all winter

notes: Choose varieties by season: spring, summer, autumn, winter types give year-round supply. Net against pigeons and cabbage white butterflies.

inGround.commonProblems: Net against butterflies and pigeons. Firm soil well.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Tall stems (90cm+) and 60cm spacing far exceed pocket capacity. Needs very firm ground and staking.

varieties.name: Bosworth
varieties.notes: F1 hybrid, tight buttons, good disease resistance

notes: Plant in very firm soil and stake. Harvest from the bottom up. Flavour improves after frost. Net against pigeons.

inGround.commonProblems: Plant very firmly and stake. Harvest from bottom up.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Baby turnips work well in pockets. Pull at golf-ball size before they outgrow the pocket depth.

varieties.name: Purple Top Milan
varieties.notes: Flat roots, very quick, distinctive colour

notes: Fast-growing. Pull young as baby turnips for sweetest flavour. Older roots become woody and hot. Leaves also edible as turnip tops.

inGround.commonProblems: Flea beetle attacks seedlings. Cover with fleece.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Massive trailing vines spread 2m+. Fruit far too heavy for pockets. Needs large ground area.

varieties.name: Crown Prince
varieties.notes: Blue-grey skin, superb flavour, stores well

notes: Plant on compost heap for extra fertility. Cure in sun for 10 days after harvest for long storage. Pinch out growing tips after 3 fruits set.

inGround.commonProblems: Massive space requirement. Train over structures.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Requires constantly moist, rich soil and is typically trenched or wrapped for blanching. Difficult in pockets.
notes: Self-blanching types are easiest. Needs very consistent watering — any dry spell causes stringy, bitter stalks. Start indoors as slow to germinate.

inGround.commonProblems: Needs constant moisture. Self-blanching types easiest.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Large bushy plants with heavy fruit. Compact patio varieties may work in top tiers with support.

varieties.name: Redskin
varieties.notes: Dwarf patio variety, no staking needed

varieties.name: Marconi Rosso
varieties.notes: Long sweet Italian type, heavy cropper

notes: Start very early indoors (January-February) for UK season. Needs warmest, most sheltered spot. Green peppers are just unripe — leave for red.

inGround.commonProblems: Needs very long warm growing season. Start early under glass.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Heavy fruit and large root system. Compact varieties may manage in top tiers but yields will be limited.
notes: Needs long warm season — start indoors January. Greenhouse ideal in Surrey. Restrict to 4-5 fruits per plant for best size.

inGround.commonProblems: Needs hottest position available. Grow under glass in UK.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Deep-rooted perennial requiring permanent raised beds. Crowns need 30cm+ depth. Completely unsuitable for pockets.
notes: Long-term investment — do not harvest for first 2 years. Plant crowns in trenches. Productive for 20+ years once established.

inGround.commonProblems: Do not harvest for first 2 years after planting.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Woody shrub that eventually grows large. Young plants fit pockets but will outgrow them within a season.

varieties.name: Miss Jessopp's Upright
varieties.notes: Columnar habit, good for containers initially

varieties.name: Prostrate Rosemary
varieties.notes: Trailing habit, ideal for walls and edges

notes: Evergreen perennial. Hates wet feet — needs excellent drainage. Better as a permanent garden shrub than container plant long-term.

inGround.commonProblems: Hates wet feet. Needs excellent drainage.

soil.notes: Mediterranean herbs prefer lean alkaline soil with good drainage.

greenstalkNotes: Young plants work well in pockets with good drainage. Prune regularly to prevent it becoming leggy.
notes: Evergreen sub-shrub. Replace every 3-4 years as plants become woody. Blue flowers attract bees. Harvest lightly in first year.

inGround.commonProblems: Becomes woody after 3-4 years. Replace or take cuttings.

soil.notes: Mediterranean herbs prefer lean alkaline soil with good drainage.

greenstalkNotes: Shallow roots and quick growth cycle perfect for pockets. Succession sow for continuous supply.

varieties.name: Confetti
varieties.notes: Feathery leaves, very slow to bolt

notes: Bolts quickly in heat — sow in partial shade and succession sow every 3 weeks. Direct sow as it hates root disturbance.

inGround.commonProblems: Bolts rapidly in heat. Sow successionally and use bolt-resistant varieties.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact varieties work in pockets. Tall flowering stems may need pinching. Do not plant near fennel — they cross-pollinate.
notes: Direct sow as dill hates transplanting. Keep away from fennel. Succession sow every 3-4 weeks. Both leaves and seeds are useful.

inGround.commonProblems: Keep away from fennel — they cross-pollinate.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Evergreen tree growing to 12m. Even small specimens far too large and deep-rooting for pockets.

varieties.name: Common Bay
varieties.notes: Standard culinary bay, can be clipped to shape

notes: Excellent in large pots clipped as standards. Protect from cold winds in severe winters. Pick leaves year-round. Slow growing.

inGround.commonProblems: Protect from cold winds and hard frost.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Herb fennel grows tall (1.5m). Florence fennel bulbs need deep soil. Both challenging in pockets.

varieties.name: Zefa Fino
varieties.notes: Florence type, bolt-resistant, good bulb formation

notes: Herb fennel (for fronds/seeds) is perennial and tall. Florence fennel (for bulbs) is annual and bolt-prone. Keep away from dill.

inGround.commonProblems: Allelopathic — inhibits growth of most nearby vegetables.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Tall canes (1.5m+) need post and wire support. Extensive suckering root system unsuitable for pockets.

varieties.name: Autumn Bliss
varieties.notes: Autumn-fruiting, cut all canes in Feb

notes: Plant bare-root canes November-March. Summer types fruit on last year's canes; autumn types on current year's growth.

inGround.commonProblems: Net against birds. Remove fruited canes after harvest.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Extremely vigorous trailing canes need 2.5m+ wire support. Far too large for any container system.
notes: Thornless varieties much easier to manage. Train on wires or against walls. Prune out fruited canes after harvest.

inGround.commonProblems: Extremely vigorous — train on wires and prune hard.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Requires ericaceous (acid) compost and grows into a large shrub. Not feasible in GreenStalk pockets.

varieties.name: Bluecrop
varieties.notes: Most reliable variety for UK, heavy cropper

varieties.name: Top Hat
varieties.notes: True dwarf variety, ideal for pots

notes: Must have acid soil (pH 4.5-5.5). Use ericaceous compost in pots. Water with rainwater not tap. Net against birds.

inGround.commonProblems: Must have acid soil (pH 4.5-5.5). Use ericaceous compost.

soil.notes: Must have ericaceous compost. Test pH before planting.

greenstalkNotes: Thorny bush reaching 1.5m. Far too large for pockets. Grows well in large pots or open ground.
notes: Tolerates partial shade better than most fruit. Thin green fruits in May for cooking; leave remainder to ripen for dessert.

inGround.commonProblems: Check for sawfly caterpillars from April — they strip leaves fast.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Large spreading bush to 1.5m each way. Needs deep, rich soil. Completely unsuitable for pockets.

varieties.name: Big Ben
varieties.notes: Very large berries, good fresh eating

notes: Plant 5cm deeper than nursery mark to encourage new basal shoots. Prune out one-third of oldest wood annually after fruiting.

inGround.commonProblems: Big bud mite causes rounded buds — pick off affected buds.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Mature bush reaches 1.2m. Can be trained as cordons against walls but pockets are too small.

varieties.name: Rovada
varieties.notes: Late, very long trusses, excellent flavour

notes: Tolerates shade and north-facing walls as cordons. Unlike blackcurrants, fruit on older wood — spur prune like apples.

inGround.commonProblems: Net against birds. Spur prune like apples.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Even on M27 dwarfing rootstock, trees need large pots (50L+) and deep root run. Not suitable for pockets.

varieties.name: Braeburn
varieties.notes: Reliable cropper, good flavour, stores well

notes: M27 rootstock for smallest trees (2m). Most need a pollination partner. Plant bare-root November-March. First crop year 3-4.

inGround.commonProblems: Most need a pollination partner. M27 for smallest trees.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Tree fruit requiring deep permanent planting. Even Quince C rootstock produces 3m+ trees.

varieties.name: Conference
varieties.notes: Most reliable UK pear, partially self-fertile

notes: Quince C rootstock for smallest trees. Most need pollination partner. Flower earlier than apples so more frost-vulnerable in Surrey.

inGround.commonProblems: Flowers early so frost-vulnerable. Need pollination partner.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Even on Pixy rootstock, plum trees are substantial. No container system can support them long-term.

varieties.name: Victoria
varieties.notes: Self-fertile, dual-purpose, most popular UK plum

notes: Pixy rootstock for smallest trees. Victoria is self-fertile and ideal for Surrey. Never prune in winter — risk of silver leaf disease.

inGround.commonProblems: NEVER prune in winter — risk of silver leaf disease.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Large spreading tree, even container-grown specimens need 40L+ pots. Completely unsuitable for pockets.

varieties.name: Brown Turkey
varieties.notes: Most reliable for UK outdoors, heavy cropper

varieties.name: Brunswick
varieties.notes: Large fruit, very hardy for UK

notes: Restrict roots for best fruiting — plant against south-facing wall in Surrey with brick-lined pit. Protect embryo figs in winter.

inGround.commonProblems: Restrict roots for best fruiting — brick-lined pit or large pot.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Huge leaves and deep crown far exceed pocket dimensions. Needs permanent ground position.

varieties.name: Timperley Early
varieties.notes: Earliest variety, thin stems, good for forcing

notes: Plant crowns with bud tip at soil surface. Do not harvest in first year. Pull (do not cut) stems. ⚠️ LEAVES ARE TOXIC — stalks are edible when cooked but leaves contain oxalic acid. Teach children the difference.
toxicWarning: Rhubarb leaves are toxic — contain high levels of oxalic acid. Stalks are safe when cooked. Teach children to only eat the stalks and never the leaves.

inGround.feeding: Thick mulch of well-rotted manure annually
inGround.commonProblems: Do not harvest in first year. Pull stems, do not cut.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact habit perfect for pockets. Edible petals add colour to salads. Attracts hoverflies which eat aphids.
notes: Hardy annual that often self-seeds. Edible petals used as poor man's saffron. Excellent companion plant attracting beneficial insects.

inGround.commonProblems: Deadhead for prolonged flowering. Self-seeds freely.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Can grow large but works in pockets when young. Star-shaped blue flowers are highly attractive to bees.
notes: Cucumber-flavoured flowers and leaves. Outstanding bee plant. Self-seeds prolifically. Direct sow as hates transplanting.

inGround.commonProblems: Self-seeds prolifically. Direct sow — hates transplanting.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Dwarf varieties only. Standard sunflowers (1.5m+) are far too tall and top-heavy for pockets.

varieties.name: Teddy Bear
varieties.notes: Dwarf double at 45cm, fluffy golden heads

notes: Dwarf varieties essential for containers. Standard tall types need open ground and staking. Seeds provide food for birds.

inGround.commonProblems: Stake tall varieties. Dwarf varieties for containers.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

kidActivity: Children can race sunflowers for height — growth can hit 30cm per week. Feed birds from the ripe seedheads in autumn.

greenstalkNotes: Compact varieties work well in pockets with excellent drainage. Will persist as a perennial if protected from frost.

varieties.name: Hidcote
varieties.notes: Compact, deep purple, classic UK variety

notes: Needs excellent drainage and full sun. Trim after flowering but never cut into old wood. Outstanding bee and butterfly plant.

inGround.commonProblems: Hates wet feet and heavy clay. Raised bed or container essential.

soil.notes: Mediterranean herbs prefer lean alkaline soil with good drainage.

kidActivity: Flowers can be dried for lavender bags — an RHS-recommended family activity — or used in baking.
soilTipSurrey: Add grit to clay soil and avoid overfeeding. Surrey's London Clay needs extra drainage for lavender to thrive.

greenstalkNotes: Dwarf varieties work well in pockets. Tall varieties may flop. Excellent cut flower and pollinator plant.
notes: Hardy annual — can also be autumn-sown for earlier flowers. Excellent for pollinators and cutting. Edible petals for cake decoration.

inGround.commonProblems: Tall varieties may need support. Deadhead for longer flowering.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.

greenstalkNotes: Compact clump-forming habit works well in pockets. Dense flower heads add vibrant colour.
notes: Biennial — sow one year, flowers the next. Modern varieties like Dash flower in first year. Delightfully fragrant. Attracts butterflies.

inGround.commonProblems: Biennial — sow one year, flowers the next. Modern varieties flower in first year.

soil.notes: Grows well in most garden soils improved with organic matter.
Genuinely clay-tolerant — one of the few fragrant herbs that thrives on heavy soil

inGround.feeding: No feeding needed — too much nitrogen reduces essential oil content
inGround.commonProblems: Can be invasive — self-seeds freely. Deadhead to control spread or contain in pots.

greenstalkNotes: Compact enough for mid-tiers. Keep trimmed to prevent it overwhelming neighbours. Releases lemon scent when brushed.

varieties.name: Common Lemon Balm
varieties.notes: Classic species, vigorous, intense lemon scent

varieties.name: All Gold
varieties.notes: Golden foliage, ornamental, slightly less vigorous

notes: Outstanding clay-tolerant fragrant herb. Releases lemon-mint scent when brushed. Makes excellent herbal tea. Attracts bees aggressively. Practically indestructible once established.

soil.notes: Unlike most mints, tolerates damp clay soil well

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: Ground cover — not harvested for culinary use typically
inGround.commonProblems: Can die back in very harsh winters. Recovers from roots in spring.

greenstalkNotes: Excellent for shaded lower tiers. Forms a thread-thin emerald carpet with powerful peppermint scent when touched.

varieties.name: Corsican Mint
varieties.notes: Species — the only form available. Tiny lilac flowers June-August

notes: The best walk-on fragrance plant for Surrey clay. Forms a 1-3cm carpet that smells powerfully of peppermint when stepped on. Best between stepping stones or in shaded GreenStalk tiers.

soil.notes: Prefers alkaline conditions — add lime to acid soils. Needs sharp drainage.

inGround.commonProblems: Short-lived perennial (3-4 years) but easily replaced. Needs excellent drainage.

greenstalkNotes: Compact clump-forming habit ideal for mid or top tiers. Warm clove fragrance carries well. Edible flowers for salads.

varieties.name: Gran's Favourite
varieties.notes: RHS AGM. White with reddish-purple lacing, strong clove scent

varieties.name: Mrs Sinkins
varieties.notes: Heritage white double, intensely clove-scented, legendary fragrance

notes: Old-fashioned cottage garden fragrance — warm clove scent that carries on the air. Evergreen silvery foliage looks good year-round. Edible flowers. Excellent pollinator plant.

soil.notes: Must be grown in pots — tender, not frost-hardy. Bring indoors October-May.

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: Aromatic foliage — leaves used in cooking and potpourri
inGround.feeding: Light liquid feed monthly through summer
inGround.commonProblems: Not frost-hardy — must overwinter indoors. Every brush of the leaves releases extraordinary rose or lemon fragrance.

greenstalkNotes: Excellent for any tier as summer display May-October. Extraordinary foliage fragrance — every touch releases rose or lemon scent.

varieties.name: Attar of Roses
varieties.notes: RHS AGM. Deep rose fragrance, strong-scented leaves

varieties.name: Lemon Crispum
varieties.notes: Intense lemon scent, variegated leaves, compact

notes: Not hardy in Surrey — treat as summer patio/GreenStalk plant May-October, overwinter on a bright windowsill. The foliage fragrance is extraordinary — every brush releases perfume. Scent may repel mosquitoes.

soil.notes: Prefers alkaline soil. Tolerates poor soil.

inGround.commonProblems: Unremarkable by day — small lilac flowers. At dusk, releases one of the most intoxicating sweet-spice fragrances of any annual.

greenstalkNotes: Compact upright habit suits containers. Scatter seeds in any tier for evening fragrance. Direct sow only — hates transplanting.

varieties.name: Night-Scented Stock
varieties.notes: Species form — small lilac flowers, extraordinary dusk fragrance

varieties.name: Starlight Scentsation
varieties.notes: Mix of pinks, purples, and whites, same powerful evening scent

notes: The classic evening-scented annual. Unremarkable by day but at dusk releases an intoxicating sweet-spice fragrance. Sow direct in April-May. Dirt cheap seeds — scatter everywhere near seating areas and paths.

soil.notes: One of the few fragrant annuals that actually enjoys moisture-retentive clay soil

inGround.feeding: Rich soil with organic matter. Liquid feed fortnightly in summer.
inGround.commonProblems: Half-hardy — raise under glass, plant out after last frost. Slugs love young plants. Towering 1.5m stems need some shelter.

greenstalkNotes: Far too tall at 1.5m for GreenStalk pockets. Grow in ground or large containers.

varieties.name: Nicotiana sylvestris
varieties.notes: RHS AGM. Pure white trumpets, 1.5m, intensely fragrant at dusk

varieties.name: Nicotiana mutabilis
varieties.notes: Shorter at 90cm, pink-to-white flowers, lighter scent

notes: Towering white trumpet flowers that look spectacular by day and smell incredible at dusk. One of the few fragrant annuals that thrives on clay. Major moth attractor. Architectural statement plant for back of border.

soil.notes: Struggles on heavy clay — grow in containers or GreenStalk with free-draining mix

inGround.feeding: No feeding — poor soil produces stronger fragrance
inGround.commonProblems: Heavy clay is unsuitable — raised bed or container only. 'Treneague' is non-flowering (for fragrant ground cover only).

greenstalkNotes: Excellent for top tier with free-draining mix. Apple-sweet fragrance released when brushed. Traditional 'plant doctor' companion.

varieties.name: Treneague
varieties.notes: Non-flowering, 5-10cm, mat-forming — for fragrant ground cover

varieties.name: Double-Flowered
varieties.notes: 15-25cm, produces flowers for chamomile tea, same fragrance

notes: Traditional 'plant doctor' said to improve the health of nearby plants. Apple-sweet fragrance released when walked on or brushed. Makes excellent herbal tea (double-flowered form). Needs free-draining soil.

soil.notes: Borderline hardy in Surrey — grow against a warm south-facing wall or in a large pot

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: Leaves for herbal tea and cooking
inGround.commonProblems: Borderline hardy (H4) — may need winter protection in cold snaps. Deciduous — loses leaves in winter but regrows from base.

greenstalkNotes: Too large for GreenStalk pockets — reaches 1.5-2m. Use a large pot near a south wall.

varieties.name: Common Lemon Verbena
varieties.notes: Species — the most powerfully citrus-scented herb available

notes: The most intensely lemon-scented plant you can grow. When rubbed, the leaves release an almost electric citrus fragrance that no other herb matches. Makes outstanding herbal tea. Grow against a warm south-facing wall in Surrey.

soil.notes: Excellent in pots. Tolerates clay if drainage is good. Drought-tolerant once established.

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: Ornamental; may fruit in warm conservatory
inGround.commonProblems: Needs winter protection below -5°C. May not fruit in UK without warm conservatory or south wall.

greenstalkNotes: Too large and slow-growing for GreenStalk. Best in a large pot in the conservatory or on a sunny patio.

varieties.name: Arbequina
varieties.notes: Compact, self-fertile, most likely to fruit in UK conservatory

varieties.name: Cipressino
varieties.notes: Narrow columnar habit, architectural, very hardy

notes: Beautiful architectural plant for a conservatory or sheltered patio. Evergreen silver-green leaves. Tolerates filtered light better than citrus. May produce small olives in a warm conservatory after 3-5 years. Hardy to -10°C when established.

soil.notes: Use citrus-specific compost. Needs acidic soil — do NOT use garden soil or multi-purpose.

inGround.feeding: Citrus feed fortnightly spring-summer, monthly autumn-winter
inGround.commonProblems: Needs minimum 10°C in winter — conservatory essential in Surrey. Leaf drop if temperature fluctuates. Yellow leaves = overwatering or iron deficiency.

greenstalkNotes: Too large and requires specific citrus compost. Must overwinter in conservatory.

varieties.name: Meyer
varieties.notes: Most cold-tolerant citrus, sweeter fruit, compact — best for UK conservatory

varieties.name: Improved Dwarf
varieties.notes: Stays under 1.5m in pot, fruits reliably in warm conservatory

notes: Challenging but rewarding in a Surrey conservatory. Needs 6+ hours of light (supplement with grow light in winter). Move outside to sunny patio May-September. Fragrant flowers are a bonus. Meyer lemon is the most forgiving variety for UK conditions.

soil.notes: Thrives in shade and damp conditions. Add leaf mould or compost.

inGround.commonProblems: None — one of the most trouble-free garden plants. Deciduous but reliable.

greenstalkNotes: Too large and shade-dependent for GreenStalk.

varieties.name: Polystichum setiferum
varieties.notes: Soft shield fern — evergreen, elegant arching fronds

notes: Perfect for the darkest corners of a conservatory or shady border. Requires zero sun. Elegant architectural foliage. Native to UK — fully hardy and maintenance-free.
kidActivity: Children find the spring unfurling of fiddleheads mesmerising. The young fiddleheads are edible (taste between asparagus, broccoli, and spinach).

soil.notes: Grows in almost any soil including heavy clay. Deep roots break up compacted subsoil and mine minerals.

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: 4-5 cuts of leaves per season for mulch/feed
inGround.feeding: None needed — it feeds everything else
inGround.commonProblems: Extremely vigorous — very difficult to remove once established. Use Bocking 14 cultivar (sterile, no self-seeding). GROW IN A POT if renting.

greenstalkNotes: Far too large and vigorous for GreenStalk. Grow in a large pot or dedicated corner.

varieties.name: Bocking 14
varieties.notes: Sterile cultivar — won't self-seed. THE one to grow. High potassium leaves.

notes: The ultimate soil improver. Deep taproot mines potassium, nitrogen, and phosphorus from subsoil. Cut leaves 4-5 times/season for: (1) liquid feed — steep in water for 4 weeks, (2) mulch around tomatoes and fruit, (3) compost activator. MUST grow Bocking 14 cultivar (sterile). RENTER WARNING: Grow in a large pot — near-impossible to remove from ground.

soil.notes: Fixes atmospheric nitrogen into the soil. Dig in before flowering as green manure.

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: Green manure — adds ~50g nitrogen per m² when dug in
inGround.feeding: None needed — it IS the feed
inGround.commonProblems: Can become invasive if allowed to self-seed. Cut and dig in before seed heads form.

greenstalkNotes: Can be used as a winter cover crop in empty GreenStalk pockets to fix nitrogen between seasons.

varieties.name: Common Crimson
varieties.notes: Beautiful red flower spikes. Sow autumn for spring nitrogen boost.

notes: Sow as a green manure after clearing summer crops. Fixes nitrogen from the air into the soil via root nodules. Dig in before flowering (about 8 weeks after sowing). Beautiful crimson flower spikes attract pollinators. Also works as a winter cover crop in GreenStalk pockets between seasons.

soil.notes: Dense root network breaks up compacted soil and improves drainage. Excellent for clay.

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: Green manure — improves soil structure, attracts beneficial insects
inGround.commonProblems: Frost-tender (H3) — dies back in autumn which is ideal for digging in as green manure.

greenstalkNotes: Quick filler for empty pockets — attracts pollinators to the tower.

varieties.name: Common Phacelia
varieties.notes: Lavender-blue flowers, the #1 bee plant in the UK

notes: The single best green manure for Surrey clay soil. Grows fast (6-8 weeks to flower), breaks up compacted clay with its dense root network, and is THE top-rated bee and pollinator plant in the UK. Not related to any vegetable family so fits anywhere in crop rotation. Sow on any bare soil March-September, dig in before flowering or let it bloom for the bees.

greenstalkNotes: Grows 60-120cm tall — too large for GreenStalk pockets.

varieties.name: Sensation Mix
varieties.notes: Classic daisy-form, white and pink, 90cm tall

varieties.name: Purity
varieties.notes: Pure white single flowers, elegant cut flower

notes: Grows from seed to flower in just 12 weeks — easy and satisfying for children. Dead-head regularly for continuous blooming. Attracts pollinators. The RHS calls it 'easy and satisfying to raise.'

inGround.feeding: No feeding — poor soil produces more flowers
inGround.commonProblems: Rich soil produces foliage at the expense of flowers. Stake tall varieties.

soil.notes: Like nasturtiums, cosmos flower best in poor soil. Avoid overfeeding.

kidActivity: Seed to flower in 12 weeks — fast enough to keep children engaged. Great for cutting gardens and bouquets.

greenstalkNotes: Tuberous perennial — needs ground planting or large container.

varieties.name: Chocamocha
varieties.notes: Compact form, rich chocolate scent, ideal for containers

notes: Remarkable chocolate-vanilla scent — a huge hit with children. This is a tuberous perennial (not grown from seed like regular cosmos). Plant tubers in spring, lift and store over winter in cold areas. The RHS School Gardening sensory guide calls it 'a big hit with the kids.'

inGround.feeding: Monthly liquid feed during growing season
inGround.commonProblems: Not fully hardy — lift tubers in autumn and store frost-free, or mulch heavily.

soil.notes: Needs good drainage. Add grit to clay soil.

kidActivity: The chocolate-vanilla scent is a major sensory hit — children love smelling the flowers. Great for sensory gardens.
soilTipSurrey: Add grit to Surrey clay for drainage. Lift tubers in autumn or mulch heavily.

greenstalkNotes: Compact enough for pockets. Fruits in partial shade — good for lower tiers.

varieties.name: Alexandria
varieties.notes: Most popular everbearing alpine, intense flavour, no runners

varieties.name: Mignonette
varieties.notes: Small intensely aromatic berries, classic variety

notes: UK native that fruits reliably even in light shade, producing small, intensely aromatic berries from June through early autumn. Spreads by runners, teaching children about plant propagation. The RHS says they can be 'left to fend for themselves.'

inGround.commonProblems: Fruit is small — manage expectations. Plant along path edges for easy picking.

soil.notes: Tolerates most soils. Woodland-edge plant that prefers some organic matter.

kidActivity: Children love foraging for tiny, intensely sweet berries among the leaves. Runners teach propagation — pot up babies as gifts.

greenstalkNotes: Grows to 90cm+ — far too large for GreenStalk pockets.

varieties.name: Common Sweet Cicely
varieties.notes: UK native woodland herb, entire plant edible, aniseed flavour

notes: Native woodland-edge herb featured at the Kitchen Garden at RHS Bridgewater. Seed pods have a clean aniseed flavour and can be 'eaten raw straight from the plant' per the RHS. The fern-like foliage is beautiful and tactile, and the whole plant — leaves, seeds, roots — is edible.

inGround.expectedYieldPerM2: Herb/ornamental — harvest leaves and seeds as needed
inGround.commonProblems: Seeds need cold stratification to germinate — sow in autumn or cold-treat seed. Self-seeds freely once established.

soil.notes: Woodland-edge conditions — moist, organic-rich soil in dappled shade.

kidActivity: Entire plant is edible — children can eat the aniseed-flavoured seeds straight from the plant. Beautiful fern-like foliage is tactile.

greenstalkNotes: Grows to 1.5m tall with vase-shaped crowns — needs open ground in shade.

varieties.name: Common Shuttlecock
varieties.notes: Native woodland fern, dramatic vase-shaped crowns to 1.5m, edible fiddleheads

notes: The showpiece shade plant. Creates dramatic, vase-shaped crowns up to 1.5m tall. Young unfurling 'fiddleheads' are edible — taste between asparagus, broccoli, and spinach. Children find the spring unfurling mesmerising. The RHS features it as a shade-loving edible.

inGround.commonProblems: Must not dry out — plant in rich, moist soil with generous organic matter.

soil.notes: Needs permanently moist, humus-rich soil. Add generous organic matter. Never let it dry out.

kidActivity: Children find the spring unfurling of fiddleheads mesmerising. Young fiddleheads are edible — taste between asparagus, broccoli, and spinach.

greenstalkNotes: Shade-dependent perennial — needs ground planting in shade.

varieties.name: Brilliance
varieties.notes: RHS award-winner. Fronds emerge copper-pink, mature to glossy green, bright orange-red spores beneath in autumn

notes: One of the RHS's ten award-winning starter ferns. Colour-changing magic: fronds emerge copper-pink in spring, mature to glossy green, and display bright orange-red spores beneath in autumn. Semi-evergreen in mild Surrey winters.

inGround.commonProblems: None — very trouble-free. Semi-evergreen in mild areas.

soil.notes: Thrives in shade and damp conditions. Add leaf mould or compost.

kidActivity: Watch fronds change colour through the seasons — copper-pink in spring, green in summer, orange-red spores in autumn. Nature's colour wheel.

greenstalkNotes: Large foliage clumps need ground planting. Also very slug-prone — GreenStalk elevation won't fully protect.

varieties.name: Sum and Substance
varieties.notes: Giant chartreuse leaves, slug-resistant, up to 1m across

notes: Bold, sculptural foliage for shade. Eaten as a vegetable in Japan — rolled-up spring 'hostons' can be harvested and fried. Children love stroking the huge leaves and watching water bead across them. Raised containers help significantly with slug protection.

inGround.feeding: General fertiliser in spring, mulch annually
inGround.commonProblems: Slugs are the main enemy. Use wool pellets, copper tape, beer traps, or raised containers.

soil.notes: Rich soil in shade. Add organic matter annually.

toxicWarning: Hostas are toxic to dogs and cats due to saponins. Skip these if you have pets.
kidActivity: Children love stroking the huge sculptural leaves and watching water bead across them.

greenstalkNotes: Compact, shade-tolerant, and colourful — works well in lower tiers with less light.

varieties.name: Super Elfin Mix
varieties.notes: Compact, huge colour range, disease resistant

notes: Delivers vivid colour in deep shade where little else blooms. The real magic for children is the exploding seed pods — ripe pods burst dramatically when touched, which is how the genus got its name (Impatiens means 'impatient'). Plant out after all frost risk passes.

inGround.commonProblems: Very frost-tender — don't plant out until late May. Downy mildew can be a problem — choose resistant varieties.

soil.notes: Keep moist but not waterlogged. Rich soil produces better flowering.

kidActivity: The exploding seed pods are irresistible — ripe pods burst dramatically when touched. Children can collect and sow seeds. Impatiens means 'impatient'!

greenstalkNotes: Can fill empty pockets in partial shade but self-seeding habit is messy in containers.

varieties.name: Blue Ball
varieties.notes: Compact, dense sky-blue flowers, ideal for edging

notes: UK native that self-seeds freely in shady spots, creating carpets of sky-blue flowers requiring almost zero maintenance. Children love the tiny, perfect blooms and the story behind the name. Near-zero maintenance once established.

inGround.commonProblems: Self-seeds prolifically — can become invasive. Pull unwanted seedlings. Biennial — sow each year or let self-seed.

soil.notes: Thrives in most conditions. Prefers moist shade but tolerates most situations.

kidActivity: Children love the tiny, perfect sky-blue blooms. Tell them the legend behind the name. Self-seeds freely — 'plant once, enjoy forever'.
